revision.text:
{{神社 | 名称 = 和歌山県護国神社 | 画像 = | 所在地 = [[和歌山県]][[和歌山市]]一番丁3 | 位置 = | 祭神 = 明治戊辰の役以来国難に殉じた和歌山県出身の戦没者36,670柱の英霊 | 創建 = | 社格 = [[護国神社|内務大臣指定護国神社]] | 本殿 = | 例祭 = 5月5日・10月5日 }} '''和歌山県護国神社'''（わかやまけんごこくじんじゃ、旧字表記：'''和歌山縣護國神社'''）は、[[和歌山県]][[和歌山市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）。[[和歌山城]]の敷地内にある。
== 歴史 == [[戊辰戦争]]以来の戦いで戦死した和歌山県出身の戦没者の霊を奉慰するため、[[1880年]]（[[明治]]13年）から[[1895年]]（明治28年）までは年1回和歌山市岡公園内の天妃山で、[[1896年]]（明治29年）からは[[和歌山城]]砂ノ丸において[[和歌山県知事]]が祭主となって毎年5月5日に招魂祭が執行されていた。
そのような中で和歌山県にも招魂社を建立しようとの動きが起きると、[[1928年]]（[[昭和]]3年）に入り和歌山県招魂社建設期成会が発足した。その敷地として、[[1937年]]（昭和12年）に[[江戸時代]]からの神域であった現在地が和歌山市から期成会に譲渡されて和歌山県招魂社が創建された。同年6月11日には社殿および社務所が建立されている。
[[1939年]]（昭和14年）4月1日に[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]令により、全国一斉に招魂社は[[護国神社]]と改称され、当社も和歌山県護国神社に名称を改めて[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定神社となる。
[[1945年]]（昭和20年）7月9日、10日に行われた[[和歌山大空襲]]で和歌山城の[[天守]]は焼失したが、当社は社務所が炎上したにとどまった。
[[1987年]]（昭和62年）11月17日、不審火により社殿が全焼したが、[[1990年]]（[[平成]]2年）に再建された。
== 祭神 == * 主祭神 - 明治戊辰の役以来国難に殉じた和歌山県出身の戦没者36,670柱の英霊
== 境内 == * 本殿 - [[1990年]]（[[平成]]2年）再建。 * 祝詞殿 - 1990年（平成2年）再建。 * 拝殿 - 1990年（平成2年）再建。 * 社務所 * 神饌所 * 祭器庫 * [[歩兵第61連隊|歩兵第六十一聯隊]]鎮魂碑 * [[歩兵第218連隊|歩兵第二百十八聯隊]]礎之碑 * [[歩兵第230連隊|歩兵第二百三十聯隊]]魂之碑 * [[満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]]顕彰碑 * 円卓と椅子 - コンクリート製。もともとは歩兵第六十一聯隊の酒保にあった藤棚の下に置かれていたもの。
== 祭事 == * 例祭 - 5月5日・10月5日
== 参考文献 == * 和歌山県護国神社由緒
==外部リンク== * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ 和歌山県護国神社]

== 歴史 == [[戊辰戦争]]以来の戦いで戦死した和歌山県出身の戦没者の霊を奉慰するため、[[1880年]]（[[明治]]13年）から[[1895年]]（明治28年）までは年1回和歌山市岡公園内の天妃山で、[[1896年]]（明治29年）からは[[和歌山城]]砂ノ丸において[[和歌山県知事]]が祭主となって毎年5月5日に招魂祭が執行されていた。
そのような中で和歌山県にも招魂社を建立しようとの動きが起きると、[[1928年]]（[[昭和]]3年）に入り和歌山県招魂社建設期成会が発足した。その敷地として、[[1937年]]（昭和12年）に[[江戸時代]]からの神域であった現在地が和歌山市から期成会に譲渡されて和歌山県招魂社が創建された。同年6月11日には社殿および社務所が建立されている。
[[1939年]]（昭和14年）4月1日に[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]令により、全国一斉に招魂社は[[護国神社]]と改称され、当社も和歌山県護国神社に名称を改めて[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定神社となる。
[[1945年]]（昭和20年）7月9日、10日に行われた[[和歌山大空襲]]で和歌山城の[[天守]]は焼失したが、当社は社務所が炎上したにとどまった。
[[1987年]]（昭和62年）11月17日、不審火により社殿が全焼したが、[[1990年]]（[[平成]]2年）に再建された。
== 祭神 == * 主祭神 - 明治戊辰の役以来国難に殉じた和歌山県出身の戦没者36,670柱の英霊
== 境内 == * 本殿 - [[1990年]]（[[平成]]2年）再建。 * 祝詞殿 - 1990年（平成2年）再建。 * 拝殿 - 1990年（平成2年）再建。 * 社務所 * 神饌所 * 祭器庫 * [[歩兵第61連隊|歩兵第六十一聯隊]]鎮魂碑 * [[歩兵第218連隊|歩兵第二百十八聯隊]]礎之碑 * [[歩兵第230連隊|歩兵第二百三十聯隊]]魂之碑 * [[満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]]顕彰碑 * 円卓と椅子 - コンクリート製。もともとは歩兵第六十一聯隊の酒保にあった藤棚の下に置かれていたもの。
== 祭事 == * 例祭 - 5月5日・10月5日

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|{{PAGENAME}}|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

revision.comment: Moved page from [[wikipedia:en:Draft:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] ([[Edithistory:|history]])
revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|{{PAGENAME}}|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|{{PAGENAME}}|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|{{PAGENAME}}|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Wikipedia drafts]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }} '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * Annual festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }} '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * Annual festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * Annual festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * Annual festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }} '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * Annual festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]][[fr:User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. revision.comment: standardizing the infoboxes
revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }}[[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }} '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * Annual festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]][[fr:User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }} '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * Annual festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]][[fr:User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }} '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * Annual festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]][[fr:User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages_from_Simple_English_Wikipedia]][[Category:Pages_from_Simple_English_Wikipedia]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }} '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * Annual festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{Shinto Navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]][[fr:User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * Annual festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[category:Pages that were the oldest for a bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{shinto-stub}}

It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * Annual festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * Annual festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the
