revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }}[[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}} {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }}[[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Wakayama Gokoku revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }}[[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{citation needed|Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}} {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] }} (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }}[[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{citation needed|Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}} {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] {{citation sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] }} In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history {{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }}[[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{citation needed|Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }}[[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{citation needed|Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted Shrine}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{citation needed|Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero {{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }}[[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{citation needed|Wakayamaken Gokoku revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[en:draft:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}}{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }}[[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{citation needed|Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation revision.comment: Adding [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]][[en:draft:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}}{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = 和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg|300px]] | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja}} | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 }}[[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{Interlanguage link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple||ja|和歌山県護国神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|ja|和歌山市|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is situated within the grounds of {{ill|Wakayama Castle|ja|和歌山城}}. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}, annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) {{citation needed|Wakayamaken Gokoku Jinja}} {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama City]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| {{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}{{citation needed|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:Gokoku
