Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|34.227455|N|135.172195|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]] | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja]] | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}} }} {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple|ja|和歌山県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[:en:Potsdam Declaration|Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[:en:en:List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[:en:Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[:en:Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[:en:Sovereignty|Sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is situated within the grounds of [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]]. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]], annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor (Japan)|qq=}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama air raids|qq=}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|qq=}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{shinto-stub}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|和歌山県護国神社|version=89611591|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Everybody linked]] {{everybodywiki link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama]] {{wikidata link|Q123110573}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

'''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is situated within the grounds of [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]]. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]], annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor (Japan)|qq=}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama air raids|qq=}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|qq=}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. [[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q123110573]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|34.227455|N|135.172195|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]] | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja]] | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}} }} {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple|ja|和歌山県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[:en:Potsdam Declaration|Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[:en:en:List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[:en:Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[:en:Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[:en:Sovereignty|Sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is situated within the grounds of [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]]. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]], annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor (Japan)|qq=}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama air raids|qq=}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|qq=}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{shinto-stub}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is situated within the grounds of [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]]. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]], annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor (Japan)|qq=}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama air raids|qq=}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|qq=}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[:en:Potsdam Declaration|Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[:en:en:List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[:en:Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[:en:Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[:en:Sovereignty|Sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is situated within the grounds of [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]]. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]], annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor (Japan)|qq=}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama air raids|qq=}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|qq=}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{shinto-stub}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[:en:Potsdam Declaration|Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[:en:en:List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[:en:Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[:en:Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[:en:Sovereignty|Sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is situated within the grounds of [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]]. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]], annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor (Japan)|qq=}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama air raids|qq=}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|qq=}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[:en:Potsdam Declaration|Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[:en:en:List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[:en:Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[:en:Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[:en:Sovereignty|Sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is situated within the grounds of [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]]. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]], annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor (Japan)|qq=}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama air raids|qq=}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|qq=}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{shinto-stub}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|和歌山県護国神社|version=89611591|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Everybody linked]] {{everybodywiki link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Wakayama]] {{wikidata link|Q123110573}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[:en:Potsdam Declaration|Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[:en:en:List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[:en:Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[:en:Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[:en:Sovereignty|Sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is situated within the grounds of [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]]. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]], annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor (Japan)|qq=}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|qq=}} (Manshū and revision.text:
[[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q123110573]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|34.227455|N|135.172195|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]] | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja]] | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}} }} {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple|ja|和歌山県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[:en:Potsdam Declaration|Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[:en:en:List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[:en:Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[:en:Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[:en:Sovereignty|Sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is situated within the grounds of [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]]. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]], annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor (Japan)|qq=}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama air raids|qq=}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|qq=}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{shinto-stub}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|和歌山県護国神社|version=89611591|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Everybody linked]] {{everybodywiki link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is situated within the grounds of [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]]. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]], annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor (Japan)|qq=}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|34.227455|N|135.172195|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]] | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja]] | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}} }} {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple|ja|和歌山県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[:en:Potsdam Declaration|Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[:en:en:List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[:en:Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[:en:Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[:en:Sovereignty|Sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is situated within the grounds of [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]]. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]], annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor (Japan)|qq=}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama air raids|qq=}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|qq=}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{shinto-stub}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|和歌山県護国神社|version=89611591|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[Category:Everybody linked]] {{everybodywiki link|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] It is situated within the grounds of [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]]. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]], annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor (Japan)|qq=}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama air raids|qq=}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|qq=}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja] (Wakayama Prefecture Jinja-chō) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{shinto-stub}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:和歌山県護国神社]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:和歌山県護国神社の鳥居 2011.7.15 - panoramio.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|34.227455|N|135.172195|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja (Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = Torii and worship hall (in the background) | location = Ichibancho 3, [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]] | deity = 36,670 war dead (Eirei) from Wakayama Prefecture who died in national crises since the Boshin War | founded = | type = [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja]] | main_hall= | festival = May 5 & October 5 | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}} }} {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|simple|ja|和歌山県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[:en:Potsdam Declaration|Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[:en:en:List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[:en:Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[:en:Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[:en:Sovereignty|Sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (和歌山県護国神社, Wakayama Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is situated within the grounds of [[:en:Wakayama Castle|Wakayama Castle]]. == History == To honor and console the spirits of those from Wakayama Prefecture who died in conflicts since the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]], annual shōkon (spirit-inviting) rites were conducted by the {{ill|Governor (Japan)|ja|地方長官|lt=Prefectural Governor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Governor (Japan)|qq=}} of Wakayama. From 1880 (Meiji 13) to 1895 (Meiji 28), these ceremonies took place once a year at Tennhi-yama in Okayama Park, Wakayama City. From 1896 (Meiji 29), they were held at Sunanomaru in Wakayama Castle on May 5 each year. A movement arose to establish a dedicated shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) in Wakayama Prefecture. In 1928 (Shōwa 3), the Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha Construction Promotion Association (Kiseikai) was formed. In 1937 (Shōwa 12), the city of Wakayama transferred land—formerly a shrine precinct since the Edo period—to the Kiseikai, and Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was built. On June 11 that year, the shrine buildings and administrative office were completed. On April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14), an Interior Ministry ordinance changed all shōkonsha nationwide into Gokoku Jinja. Thus, Wakayama Prefecture Shōkonsha was renamed “Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja,” becoming an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku shrine. During the {{ill|Wakayama air raids|ja|和歌山大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama air raids|qq=}} of July 9–10, 1945 (Shōwa 20), Wakayama Castle’s tenshu (keep) was burned down, but the shrine only lost its administrative office. In 1987 (Shōwa 62), a suspicious fire destroyed the shrine buildings, but they were rebuilt by March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). == Enshrined Deities == * Primary enshrined deities: 36,670 spirits (Eirei) of Wakayama Prefecture who died in service to the nation from the Meiji Restoration onward. == Grounds == * Honden (main hall) - Rebuilt March 29, 1992 (Heisei 4). * Norito-den (hall of blessing) - Rebuilt 1992. * Haiden (worship hall) - Rebuilt 1992. * Shamusho (office) * Shinsenjo (food offering hall) * Saikiko (ceremonial instrument storehouse) * Memorials for various army regiments (61st, 218th, 230th Infantry Regiments) * Memorial monument for {{ill|Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun|qq=}} (Manshū and Mongolia Youth Pioneer Volunteer Army) * Round table and chairs (made of concrete) - Originally in the canteen area of the 61st Infantry Regiment’s wisteria trellis. == Festivals == * festivals: May 5, October 5 == References == * Wakayama-ken Gokoku Jinja official history == External Links == * [http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1001/ Wakayama-ken Gokoku
